Management Concepts  3 
 
Part 1 
Management Overview 
For the management section of the RD exam, don’t be overwhelmed by the terms. Step one of 
being ready for this part of the test is being familiar with these terms and their definitions. Note 
that I didn’t say “knowing the definitions”, I said “be familiar with”. You don’t need to be able to 
write an essay about systems theory, but you should be able to recognize a definition referring 
to inputs, transformation, and outputs, as a key part of systems theory. Before we jump into the 
5 Management Functions, let’s do an overview. This overview isn’t something to skim over – 
many of these overview ideas are tested on the exam. Plus, this overview provides context.  
First, it’s important to understand that systems theory and systems approach, functional 
organization, and management functions have similar concepts, but are not directly connected. 
They are distinct theories that are used together in practice.  
The systems approach uses functional subsystems and the transformation of inputs to outputs. 
While the management functions are related to the systems approach, they are not directly 
involved in transformation and they are not linking processes between communications and 
decision making. 
The ideas are separate but similar. 
Systems Approach and Functional 
Organization 
 Management Functions 
• Functional subsystems 
• Linking processes 
• Inputs, outputs, and transformation 
 
• Planning, organization, staffing, directing, 
controlling 
• Used to coordinate subsystems 
Procurement, production, distribution and service, sanitation, and maintenance are the 
functional subsystems within the foodservice system, each classified by function. Come back 
to this overview when we talk about functional organization and systems approach in Part II. 
Don’t worry, I put a reminder in Part II for you. 
The 5 management functions are planning, organization, staffing, directing, and controlling.
1
 
Planning and other management functions set plans and objectives, but they don’t outline 
exactly how the objectives should be achieved. Furthermore, the 5 management functions don’t 
change between departments or between subsystems. Managers in each department apply the 
same 5 management functions – meaning that the 5 management functions are pervasive. 
As you go through the details of each management function, build a framework in your mind on 
which to organize and remember the different definitions. Instead of just brute-force memorizing 
terms and definitions on flashcards, having context around the terms will help distinguish similar 
terms and definitions from one another.  
Summarize the previous paragraph.

Management Concepts  4 
 
The 3 skills you need to be a successful manager are:
1
 
1. Technical – knowledge and capabilities to perform specific 
tasks 
2. Human/Interpersonal – soft skills that should be nurtured, 
such as communication and attention to relationships (in 
contrast to hard skills that can be learned) 
3. Conceptual – ability to integrate activities and interests of 
an organization → the most important skill to have at higher 
levels of management 
 
 
There are 3 types of 
resources core to all 
management principles.
2
 
• Human resources 
• Physical resources 
• Financial resources 
Write out and define each of the 3 skills necessary to be a successful manager. Which is the 
most important at higher levels of management? 
What 3 types of resources are central to all management principles? 
The 5 Management Functions 
To begin, review the 5 management functions in this section. For applied management 
questions on the exam, like situational questions, you’ll need to be able to identify examples of 
each function. Keeping this framework in mind will also help you learn the other concepts.  
Management is the integration and coordination of resources to achieve the desired objectives 
of the organization. There are numerous functions, processes, methods, and theories on how to 
“do management”.  
The 5 management functions are used to coordinate subsystems in order to achieve objectives. 
Managing subsystems through the 5 functions is expected to result in the achievement of 
objectives.
1
 
Planning – using problem solving and decision making to ensure proper utilization of all 
resources 
Organization – assigning duties; delegating authority; creating responsibility 
Staffing- recruiting and screening potential employees; hiring, orienting, and training new 
employees; supervising and evaluating employees 
Directing – using leadership and management styles; using motivation and communication 
Controlling – establishing standards for performance; evaluating actual performance; 
implementing corrective action 
Can you think of a mnemonic to remember the 5 functions?  
P O S D C 
Take out a few sheets of paper and label each one with the name of one of the 5 management 
functions. As you read through the next sections, write down what you think is important to know 
about each one.

Management Concepts  5 
 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
6 
 
Planning 
The planning function of management involves looking ahead and preparing for the other 
management roles. Managers need to see the organization as it could be, not as it is. Planning 
is the primary function of management and the most basic. It involves formulating plans that 
balance needs and demands with the available resources (financial, physical, human).  
Planning includes: 
• Planning by objective 
• Facilitating coordination and controlling 
• Minimizing uncertainty 
 
 As a function, planning is: 
• Goal oriented 
• A continuous process 
• Pervasive  
 
Planning occurs at all levels of management and in all departments. 
One key resource important in planning is human resources planning which ensures that an 
organization’s (or department’s) labor requirements are met.
1
 Human resources planning 
includes forecasting staffing needs (FTEs) and analyzing labor market conditions. 
Organization 
Organization is the management function of grouping activities, delegating authority, and 
coordinating relationships (both horizontally and vertically).
 1
 
Organization includes: 
• Identifying activities to complete and to be delegated, and grouping them 
• Assigning responsibility for effective management – everyone should know who they are 
accountable to and what authority they have 
• Successful planning and organization to avoid duplication of efforts which is a waste of 
resources (e.g. time, money, staff) 
Organization is also key to maintain a safe work environment which is important to: 
• Reduce use of workman’s compensation, resulting in decreased costs 
• Reduce lost workdays, resulting in decreased costs 
• Demonstrate care for employees 
Staffing 
Staffing includes recruiting, screening, and selecting potential employees; hiring, orienting, and 
training new employees, and supervising and evaluating current employees.
 1
 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
7 
 
Hiring 
What is the difference between recruitment and selection?
 1
 
Recruitment is locating and encouraging potential applicants to apply for a job opening. 
Selection is comparing applicant knowledge, skills, and abilities to those required of a position 
and choosing the most qualified applicant. 
In hiring, the manager is responsible for writing job descriptions and job specifications. No 
matter the level of the job, a job description and specification must be written. 
What is the difference between a job description and job specification?
1
 
A job description includes job title, tasks, duties, roles, and responsibilities for a certain job – in 
sum, what the candidate can expect to do if they get the job. 
A job specification includes the desired qualifications, skills, and abilities needed to complete 
the job – in sum, the job specification characterizes the ideal candidate. 
On the day a new employee is hired, they should be given performance objectives. 
Employee Training 
The manager is also responsible for making sure there is a planned new employee 
orientation and training program in place.
1
 Using a standardized program for all new hires 
means that all employees will be familiar with policies, procedures, job tasks, expectations, and 
other important aspects of the job. It also reduces the chance of miscommunication, like an 
employee saying they didn’t know they needed to do something. 
Managers should also have planned development, which is the process of improving the 
technical, human, and conceptual skills of employees.
 1
 An example would be attendance at 
webinars, lunch and learns, or in-service trainings.  
In foodservice, managers should have planned in-service trainings that utilize the principles of 
adult learning and effective task teaching. To decide on an in-service training topic, a manager 
should observe staff and their work area to determine what topics need to be addressed, such 
as things to improve, things to work on, and things that may need to be explained more 
thoroughly. 
How should a manager decide on an in-service training topic? 
a. Choose from the job specifications 
b. Observe staff and their work area 
c. Ask a manager in another department 
d. Find ideas online 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
8 
 
Employee Performance 
Personnel policies should reflect the current realities of the specific work place. Policies should 
be kept up-to-date, and should be reinforced by strong supervision to make sure employees are 
being treated fairly and expectations are consistent.
3
  
If an employee finds job tasks too demanding, how could you, as a manager, address the 
situation? You should help the employee improve their performance by empowering them and 
motivating them to do well.
1
 Specifically, you can simplify their tasks, lessen their workload, and 
reduce time pressure, while maintaining a positive attitude and constructive conversation. You 
wouldn’t re-design a job description to match their needs and to keep them in their current 
position. Having a conversation that re-iterates the performance standards for their job 
description also wouldn’t be effective – it wouldn’t actually reduce the job demands they feel or 
improve their performance. Providing additional resources, like staff assistance, modified 
equipment, and raw materials don’t empower the employees. 
Write out the scenario from the paragraph above and the options to address it. Explain why 
each possible option is ideal or not. 
Employee Discipline 
Consistency is key when disciplining employees – an employee should feel like they are 
getting the same treatment that any other employee would for the same offense.
 1
  
Identifying solutions to correct undesirable behaviors, providing feedback on performance, and 
identifying causes for unacceptable behavior are all processes involved in employee discipline 
specific to improving outcomes, but the most important, overarching idea is consistency. 
What are 3 processes key to improving outcomes via employee discipline? 
Progressive discipline is a graduated range of responses to employee performance problems 
or conduct problems.
 1
 The idea is that disciplinary responses should be appropriate to the 
problem. Employees should know what to expect, and employers are held to fair, consistent, 
and objective standards for discipline and proper documentation of both actions and decisions. 
You may get questions on the RD exam that ask which response is most appropriate for a given 
situation. First consider what the problem in the scenario is – is it an employee’s work quality? 
tardiness? poor conduct? Then, evaluate each answer choice in terms of addressing the issue, 
appropriateness of the response, and formal documentation. Someone being tardy for the first 
time, for example, doesn’t warrant reporting that employee to the manager’s manager. 
Progressive discipline (escalation) includes these 4 steps for formal documentation: 1) verbal 
warning, 2) written warning, 3) suspension, 4) termination. The verbal warning itself would be 
documented, and if there are situations leading up to the verbal warning, those can be 
documented as well. For example, a manager might document unkempt employee appearance, 
poor availability when on call, or inappropriate conduct. These all serve as rationale for 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
9 
 
discipline. Note that informal conversations, like work-related advice from a supervisor, don’t 
require documentation, especially documentation related to discipline or the employees “file”.
 1 
 
Directing 
Directing involves the actual action steps taken from planning, organization, and staffing. 
Organizing determines which tasks should be delegated while directing actually delegates the 
tasks. Key skills for directing are soft skills (interpersonal skills).
2
 Problem solving and 
communication are particularly important in directing. 
Have you heard of “emotional intelligence”? Emotional intelligence includes a few skills, 
including emotional awareness, emotional self-regulation, communication, and 
empathy.
 4
 Emotional awareness is the ability to identify and name your own emotions. 
Emotional regulation is the ability to manage your own emotions when necessary and helping 
others to do so.  
 
Controlling 
Controlling ensures there is effective and efficient utilization of organizational resources in order 
to achieve the planned goals.
1
 Controlling serves two basic purposes: facilitating coordination 
and help in planning 
Controlling has 5 characteristics. 
• An end function – comes into play when performances are made with plans 
• Pervasive – includes all managers, all levels, all types of concerns 
• Forward looking – look to the past for effective control in the future 
• Dynamic – continuous review, making changes wherever possible 
• Related to planning – without planning, controlling is meaningless and without controlling, 
planning is useless 
What are the 4 steps in controlling?  
1) Establish standards (targets to achieve) 
2) Measure performance 
3) Compare actual and standard performance – identify causes and extent of deviation 
4) Take remedial actions – take corrective measures for deviations and possibly revising 
targets. A manager can only exercise control by taking corrective measures 
As a manager, quality improvement steps would occur during planning and controlling. 
Note that while the functions may occur in a certain order, they are not necessarily stepwise. A 
manager might do several functions within one day, one week, or in one project. 
Write out the 5 characteristics of controlling and the 4 steps in controlling.

1. Are the main management functions the same from department to department? In 
nursing and foodservice? In accounting and medical?  
 
2. Do the management functions help you create an approach on how objectives are

3. What management skill is the most important at higher levels of management? 
 
4. Contrast hard skills and soft skills. If you are hiring a candidate, would you rather have

5. What is human resources planning? 
 
6. Delegation is a key part of which management function?

9. In what management function or functions would quality improvement activities occur?

11 
 
Study Guide Questions (Answers) 
1. Are the main management functions the same from department to department? In 
nursing and foodservice? In accounting and medical?  
• Yes, management functions are pervasive. 
 
2. Do the management functions help you create an approach on how objectives are 
achieved?  
• No, they help set plans and objectives, but they don’t outline how objectives will 
be achieved. 
 
3. What management skill is the most important at higher levels of management? 
• Conceptual skills are the most important at higher levels of management. 
 
4. Contrast hard skills and soft skills. If you are hiring a candidate, would you rather have 
someone with technical expertise but poor communication, or good soft skills who needs 
to learn the “tricks of the trade”? 
• Employers often prefer to hire candidates with exemplar soft skills because they 
are typically good workers, team players, and able to learn the technical skills 
they lack. In contrast, a candidate with exemplar technical skills but poor 
communication, inability to work in a team, or poor work ethic would be difficult to 
work with and that difficulty would outweigh the benefit of them knowing the hard 
skills. Obviously, the ideal candidate has both soft and hard skills.  
 
5. What is human resources planning? 
• A key part of the planning management function, human resources planning 
includes forecasting staffing needs and analyzing labor market conditions to 
ensure that your department or organization has adequate labor requirements 
met. 
 
6. Delegation is a key part of what management function? 
• Organization. 
 
7. When should a new employee be given their performance objectives? 
• On the day they are hired. 
 
8. Why should there be a standardized new employee training that all employees go 
through? 
• A standardized program means all employees are familiar with their expectations 
and reduces the chance of miscommunication. Standardized new employee 
training and reinforced personnel policies serve the same function. Both are 
important. 
 
9. In what management function or functions would quality improvement activities occur? 
• Planning and controlling.

12 
 
The Healthcare System 
Accreditation Organizations 
Hospital accrediting agencies must acquire “deeming status” from the Centers for Medicare and 
Medicaid Services (CMS) per the Social Security Act. Accreditation is “a process by which an 
impartial organization reviews a company’s operation to ensure the company conducts business 
in a manner consistent with national standards.”
5
 CMS does not require accreditation for 
healthcare delivery professionals to receive Medicare and Medicaid reimbursement.
6
 Some 
states may have regulatory requirements that may require accreditation. Some accreditation 
agencies serve health insurance companies, some serve human and health organizations, and 
others serve healthcare delivery organizations, including hospitals. This section will focus on 
accreditation agencies for the last category. 
The Joint Commission (TJC) was established in 1951 and is an independent, not-for-profit 
organization that accredits and certifies hospitals, home care organizations, rehabilitation 
center, long term and nursing facilities, behavioral health organizations, addiction treatment and 
recovery programs, ambulatory care providers, as well as clinical laboratories.
7
 TJC has had 
deeming authority since 1966. The governing board is made up of healthcare practitioners, 
public health professionals, and public representatives. The TJC accreditation standards are 
developed according to National Patient Safety Goals and input from stakeholders and exceed 
the standards of the CMS Conditions of Participation (CoPs). The TJC accreditation survey 
process is patient-centered and focused on the healthcare delivery process on-site surveys are 
conducted every 3 years with annual self-assessments prepared by hospitals. 
DNVGL Healthcare, Inc. is a corporation and has had deeming authority for hospitals in the US 
since 2008. Their accreditation requirements relate directly to the CMS CoPs with a focus on 
continuous improvement processes and implementing CMS quality initiatives. DNVGLHC 
conducts annual surveys to validate Medicare compliance. DNVGLCHC is led and managed by 
healthcare operational experts, including leaders in healthcare management, clinical services, 
health law, ISO certification, and engineering. 
The National Committee for Quality Assurance (NCQA) is a non-profit established in 1990 to 
improve healthcare through evidence-based methods and identifying organizations that are run 
in ways which improve care. They accredit health plans and Patient-Centered Medical Homes 
and have moved to measuring the quality of medical providers using their Healthcare 
Effectiveness Data and Information Set (HEDIS) which includes data on 60% of the US 
population and provides data for accountability and benchmarking.
8
 
CMS has a role in certifying hospitals, meaning that they meet federal regulatory standards and 
thus are eligible for reimbursement from federally funded programs (e.g. Medicare). If a hospital 
is accredited by an organization with deeming authority, it is also certified by CMS, because it 
has met or exceeded CMS standards. However, an organization does not need to be accredited 
to be CMS-certified. CMS conducts investigations and surveys (through state agency partners), 
including random validation surveys of accredited hospitals, to ensure compliance and eligibility. 
Why would an organization pay a fee to be accredited when they could be certified for free by a 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
13 
 
state agency on behalf of CMS? The answer: time is money. The organization would have to 
wait for an unannounced Medicare certification survey, and during that time, they are ineligible 
for Medicare reimbursement. Alternatively, they can pay an accreditation agency with deeming 
authority from CMS, and receive eligibility sooner.
9
  
Value-based Healthcare 
Volume-based care (or fee-for-service care) is a payment model where provider 
reimbursements are tied to the quantity of services and procedures. This model incentivizes 
health systems to provide more treatments to more patients rather than providing patients with 
only the care they need or by focusing on high-quality treatment.  
In 2007, there was renewed interest and dedication in moving towards value-based care, with 
an initial focus on Patient-Centered Medical Homes, which center on team healthcare 
approaches in primary care organization and delivery.
10,11  
The Affordable Care Act (ACA) created a new division in CMS – the Center for Medicare and 
Medicaid Innovation (CMMI) – which was created to develop and test Alternative Payment 
Models (APMs). APMs are “alternative” to traditional fee-for-service care.  
One of the first APMs was accountable care organizations (ACOs), which are groups of 
healthcare professionals and/or hospitals who voluntarily come together to coordinate high-
quality care to Medicare patients. Their goal is to prevent medical errors and avoid unnecessary 
health care duplication. Provider reimbursements are tied to quality metrics and reduced costs 
of care.  
During the COVID-19 pandemic, medical providers using APMs were better able to manage 
financial volatility compared to those using traditional FFS payment models.
11  
As of 2016, the CMMI had explored 10 APMs that help advance the healthcare system from 
traditional fee-for-service care towards value-based care by tying 30% of traditional Medicare 
payments to quality or to value.
12
  
Examples include the Medicare Shared Savings Program (MSSP) created by the ACA, Next 
Generation ACOs for ACOs experienced in population health coordination, the Comprehensive 
ESRD Care Model which creates ACOs that include dialysis facilities, nephrologist, and other 
providers and suppliers to coordinate care with accountability, Bundled Payments for Care 
Improvement which targets 48 conditions within a single payment for care episodes, among 
others. 
Credentialing and Licensure 
Some states in the US have licensure laws for dietitians, meaning that to practice in that state, 
an RD needs to be issued a license by the appropriate state agency. These statues include a 
title protection provision which prevents unlicensed individuals from presenting themselves to 
the public as a dietitian.  

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
14 
 
Labor Unions and Labor Legislation 
What is a labor union? A labor union “is an organization of workers joined to protect their 
common interests and improve their working conditions...[and serve] as an intermediary 
between the employer and employees.”
13
 Labor unions in the US are legally sanctioned and 
formally organized. National and international labor unions often have union “locals” at the state 
level or smaller geographical level with charters from the larger national or international 
organization. Locals may be based on geography or company employment and are typically 
numbered. Locals have governing bodies, including an executive board, that communicate and 
uphold the interests of the larger union. Unions are run by paid staff, with their salary provided 
for by union dues. Union dues are also used for legal costs, negotiation costs, arbitrators’ fees, 
strike funds, and political activity. 
What is the purpose of a labor union? The primary purpose of labor unions is to give workers 
the power to negotiate for favorable hours, wages, and working conditions through collective 
bargaining.  
• Collective bargaining occurs when representatives from the employer (typically 
employees from the Human Resources Department) and representatives from the union 
meet to negotiate about employment conditions, wages, and hours.
13
 It is called 
collective bargaining because the union representatives are bargaining on behalf of the 
union members. This situation is in contrast, for example, to a dietitian negotiating with 
their supervisor about a promotion for only themselves. 
 
• Collective bargaining results in a collective bargaining agreement (CBA) that outlines 
the terms of employment for employees that are a member of that labor union/bargaining 
unit.
13
 The CBA is enforced and monitored by the union, with complaints registered and 
addressed through established grievance procedures and arbitration. Management 
cannot change working conditions without negotiating with union representatives.
13
 
o At-will employment means that an employer can fire you or adjust your working 
conditions at nearly any time for nearly any reason.
14
 At-will employment is in 
contrast to positions where an employer must provide just cause (as defined by 
the CBA) for termination or discipline.
 13
 
What laws govern labor unions? 
• The National Labor Relations Act (NLRA, also called the Wagner Act) was passed in 
1935 and guarantees rights of private sector employees to organize and join labor/trade 
unions, engage in collective bargaining, select their representation, and to strike if 
necessary. The NLRA protects an employee’s right to join a union as well as their right 
to not join a union. The act also created the National Labor Relations Board which 
monitor the relationship between employees, unions, and employers and enforce the 
NLRA. This federal act permits states to pass ‘right to work’ laws (also called ‘open shop 
laws’). State laws govern public sector unions. This act is pro-labor. 
o You should be familiar with the terms closed shop, open shop, agency shop, and 
union shop (next page). 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
15 
 
o Under the NLRA, employers can express a preference that employees not 
unionize but employers cannot say that employees cannot unionize.  
o Under the NLRA, unions must provide “duty of fair representation” and protect 
employee rights as outlined in the union contract. 
o The act was extended in 1974 to include employees of private nonprofit hospitals 
and nursing homes
15
 with amendments that unions representing workers in these 
industries provide 90 days’ notice before terminating labor agreements and that 
labor disputes in these industries must be submitted to mediation by a federal or 
similar agency to avoid strikes that would affect essential services.
16
 
 
• The Taft-Hartley Act (Labor Management Relations Act) was passed in 1947 and 
added a list of “unfair labor practices” on the part of unions to the NLRA. This act 
outlawed closed shops and permitted union shops in certain scenarios.
17
 It also allows 
for the US president to stop national strikes for up to 80 days. This act addressed issues 
like restraining employee rights, discriminating against employees due to union 
membership, refusing to bargain in good faith, encouraging striking to force union 
matters, and charging excessive union fees. This act is pro-management. 
 
• Labor-Management Reporting and Disclosure Act (also called the Landrum-Griffin 
Act) was passed in 1959 and, like the Taft-Hartley Act, also amended the NLRA. It was 
passed to regulate the internal affairs of labor unions by imposing a code of conduct in 
response to corruption, racketeering, and other misconduct common in unions during the 
1950s.
18
 For example, it protected union members from unions misusing dues and 
pension funds. 
Write out the name of the previously discussed 3 labor union laws in the order in which they 
were passed. Think of ways to remember what each act was about. What was the purpose of 
each act? Which were considered pro-labor and which were considered pro-management?  
Terms to know 
• A bargaining unit is a group of employees with clear and identifiable common interests. 
A union may be made up of a single bargaining unit or a union may be made up of 
several bargaining units. A single labor union must represent the bargaining unit, 
meaning that a single bargaining unit cannot be split between different unions. Some job 
positions may be classified as ‘bargaining unit positions’ meaning that the job is 
represented by a labor union and that union negotiates the conditions of that job. If you 
apply and are chosen for that position, you would not need to join the union. Conversely, 
however, if you want to join a union, you must be in a recognized bargaining unit.
19
 
 
• Closed shops refer to situations where only union members can be hired for positions 
covered by a bargaining unit. Closed shops were outlawed in 1947 by the Taft-Hartley 
Act.
13
 
 
• Union shops refer to situations where an employee doesn’t need to be a union member 
to be hired but must join the union within a certain time period after beginning work.
 13
 
Union shops are illegal in some states 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
16 
 
• Agency shops refer to situations where you do not need to be a union member to hold 
a bargaining unit position but you have to pay partial union dues and fees.
 13
 The money 
you pay only covers union activities that you benefit from, such as collective bargaining 
and grievance adjustments, but not political activity or union organization.  
o Grievance adjustment refers to the right of employees to present grievances to 
their employer and have conditions adjusted as long as those adjustments are 
not in conflict with the collective bargaining agreement.
20
  
 
• Open shops are situations where no one can be compelled to join (or not join) a union 
or to pay dues but still is considered part of the bargaining unit and benefits from union 
activities on behalf of that bargaining unit.
 13
 Open shops are typically in “right to work” 
states which is named as such because any employee has the “right to work” regardless 
of union membership. 
 
• Arbitration is a third-party intervention where the appointed arbitrator has the power to 
dictate settlement terms, guaranteeing solutions. Mediation stands in contrast to 
arbitration where mediators have no power to dictate a final ruling. 
 
Other labor-related legislation 
• The Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), originally passed in 1938, established a Federal 
minimum wage, overtime pay eligibility, and child labor standards affecting both full-time 
and part-time workers in private and public sector positions.
21
 This Act has been 
amended several times in order to raise the Federal minimum wage and extend/repeal 
protections to certain employee groups. The Act established recordkeeping regulations 
requiring employers to keep records of wages and hours worked.
 21
  
o The Equal Pay Act of 1963 amended the FLSA to make it illegal to pay workers 
lower wages strictly on the basis of sex.  
o The 1967 Age Discrimination in Employment Act prohibited employee 
discrimination against persons 40 years of age or older.  
o The 1983 Migrant and Seasonal Agricultural Worker Protection Act provided pay, 
and working condition protection for migrant and seasonal farm workers.

Mintzberg Management Roles 
Managers fill many different roles throughout the day. They switch roles as tasks, situations, 
and expectations require. These roles include interpersonal, informational, and decisional.
 1
  
Interpersonal roles provide information and include the roles of a figurehead, leader, and 
liaison.
25
 Figureheads have social, ceremonial, and legal responsibilities; they serve as an 
authority figure and a source of inspiration. Leaders serve as a leader, and manage 
performance and responsibilities. Liaisons communicate with internal and external contacts and 
network effectively. 
Informational roles process information and include the roles of a monitor, disseminator, and 
spokesperson.
25
 Monitors seek information related to their industry, look for changes in the 
industry environment, and monitor the team on productivity and well-being. Disseminators 
communicate useful information to teams and colleagues. Spokespersons represent and speak 
for their organization, and transmit information about their organization and goals to people 
outside of it. 
Decisional roles use information and include the roles of entrepreneur, disturbance handler, 
resource allocator, and negotiator.
25
 Entrepreneurs create and control change (including 
problem solving), generate new ideas, and implement them. Disturbance handlers take charge 
when the team hits an unexpected roadblock and mediate disputes within the team. Resource 
allocators determine where organizational resources should be applied, allocate funding, and 
assign staff and other resources. Negotiators take part in and direct important negotiations 
within the team, department, and even organization.  
From the Mintzberg’s Management Roles, you can see how the management functions interplay 
with the roles, which in turn interplay with other management concepts we’ve talked about so 
far. 
How do the management roles fit in with the 5 management functions? Some of the wording of 
the management roles are even similar to the language for the management functions. For 
example, “resource allocator” fits with both planning and controlling.

Leadership 
What is a leader? 
What is an effective leader? An effective leader is one who accepts that change will happen 
(and that change is a good thing) and works with others on their team to facilitate the pace and 
the outcome of that change.
1
 By working with others, and keeping a positive attitude about 
change, the effective leader empowers themselves as a leader and empowers their employees 
as teammates.  
An effective leader:
 1
 
• Copes with change – accepts that change should and will happen, working with others 
to set the pace and outcome of that change process 
• Empowers employees – empowers themselves and employees as a team to adapt and 
embrace change 
• Perseveres through difficult processes 
In contrast, an effective leader does not need to “cope with complexities” – they accept 
complexity as something that will happen and instead, cope with change to facilitate that 
environment.
 1
 Furthermore, an effective leader doesn’t seek to be understood by their team – 
they should be considered an expert and referent by their employees.
 1
  
What Mintzberg Management Role corresponds to a leader being an expert and referent? 
Not all managers are effective leaders, and within lower ranks of an organization, employees 
may be effective leaders without being a manager.  
Just as there are different leadership styles, a single leader’s style may change due to the 
situation – some styles are more effective when the goal of leadership is compliance, and other 
leadership styles are more effective when there is a time limitation. 
If the leadership goal is compliance, a leader should be seen as a role model who is legitimate, 
an expert, competent, and of a high position in the organization.
 1
  
Compliance may be the goal when change has to occur to meet new external guidelines – the 
change has to happen, and it has to happen a certain way, so having an effective leader is key 
for the process to go smoothly.

The Hersey, Blanchard, and Johnson leadership effectiveness model (commonly abbreviated as 
HBL for Hersey and Blanchard leadership model) focuses on situations rather than leadership 
styles.

The leadership effectiveness model capitalizes on this flexibility to increase a leader’s 
effectiveness. By adapting to the current situation and leading appropriately, a leader is effective 
in meeting the goal. The HBL model is centered around the leader’s decision making, and thus 
uses an individualistic decision-making approach in contrast to a group decision making 
approach.

The HBL model is adaptive and flexible. Leaders consider their team, the environment, and the 
task at-hand when making decisions. Thus, this model treats leadership as task-dependent 
and relationship-dependent. Task situation refers to an employee’s ability and relationship 
situation refers to the employee’s willingness or confidence in their ability. Put in simple steps, a 
leader using the HBL model will: 
1. Identify the category of follower (employee) 
2. Adapt leadership style and behavior to match follower (employee) 
3. Continue to assess follower (employee) status and adapt leadership style and behavior

and the relationship situation, or an employee’s willingness or confidence.

Task behavior: How knowledgeable is the employee? Do they have the necessary skills? How 
much help will they need from the leader? You can think of task behavior as the degree of 
directive behavior required by the leader. When “task behavior” or “directive behavior” is HIGH,

“relationship behavior” or “supportive behavior” is LOW, the employee is self-motivated and 
does not require a large amount of support from the leader. When “relationship behavior” or 
“supportive behavior” is HIGH, the employee requires a large amount of support from the leader 
as they may not be confident or excited about the task

M2 are inexperienced like M1, but are interested in learning. The leader should adapt their style to 
provide more supportive behavior to match the employee’s excitement about increasing their skill set. The 
leader needs to provide both direction and support.M2 maturity corresponds to R2 readiness and S2 -

M3 are employees who have most of the skills and knowledge necessary to get the job done correctly, 
thus requiring little guidance on the task from the leader. They benefit from supportive behavior to 
enhance their confidence and build the employee-leader relationship. M3 maturity corresponds to R3 
readiness and S3 – participating  
M4 are employees who are confident in their ability to complete at ask and can do so independently, 
without direction or support from the leader. M4 maturity corresponds to R4 r4e3adiness and S4 – 
delegating.

Scientific Management 
By optimizing and simplifying jobs, you can improve productivity that encourages employees 
and managers to cooperate with one another. A key aspect of scientific management, versus 
other management theories, such as continuous improvement, is that there is “one right way”. 
With other types of management theories and styles, there is not only one right way. 
Scientific management, also known as “Taylorism”, has 4 principles
33
: 
1. Replace standard procedures with a “rule of thumb” or simple habit, use the scientific method to study 
the work processes, and determine the most efficient way to perform specific tasks.

train them to work at maximum efficiency. 
3. Monitor work performance, and provide instructions and supervision to ensure that work is at its most 
efficient. 
4. Allocate the work between managers and workers so managers spend their time planning and 
training, allowing the workers to perform their tasks efficiently.

The Management Process 
The management process begins with establishing objectives and then identifies functions 
required to achieve the objectives. Then, the manager determines responsibilities – describes 
them and assigns them as appropriate. Finally, the manager delegates by assessing which 
employees have the skills and/or resources to achieve the tasks.

objectives from her director and does not have the time to accomplish them. She finds room in

new employee. In order for the employee to successfully meet these delegated responsibilities, 
they need authority along with the responsibility of the tasks. The employee does not have 
complete control over these additional objectives - they still report back to the Foodservice

Authority is the power to make decisions, such as the decision to

perform a task.

the required skills (and thus capability) or if time 
allows, train those who do not currently have the 
skills. 
A manager should delegate tasks to employees who are capable. 
Responsibility 
Responsibility is the obligation to perform an assigned activity, or see 
that someone else performs it. 
Thus, both the manager and employee are responsible for this task. The 
tasks impart responsibility upon the employee.

Accountability is being responsible for one’s self, to an organization, or 
to a manager. Both the manager and employee are accountable for the 
task. The standards of performance impact accountability as the 
employee reports back to the manager.

Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs 
According to Maslow’s theory, humans are motivated by a hierarchy of needs in that lower 
needs on a hierarchy must be met before achieving the needs higher up on the hierarchy. The 
order of needs may differ based on situations or individuals and much of human behavior is 
motivated by multiple needs. As you read through this page, fill out the Maslow’s Hierarchy 
worksheet or create your own.  
 
Basic needs include physiological needs (food, warmth, water, rest) and safety needs 
(security and safety). These are also known as deficiency needs or D-needs along with 
psychological needs. Motivations decrease as needs are met, and motivations increase as the 
duration of deficiency continues.  
Next are belonginess and love needs (intimate relationships, friends), and esteem needs 
(prestige, feeling of accomplishment)– the psychological needs, referenced above, are the 
human needs for interpersonal relationships that motivate behavior at this level. Esteem 
includes esteem for yourself (dignity, independence), desire for reputation (status, prestige) and 
desire for respect from others. Esteem needs are higher among children and adolescents than 
certain adults. 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
33 
 
The highest level of Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs is self-actualization (achieving your full 
potential) also called the self-fulfillment needs. In contrast to D-needs, self-actualization

Management Theories 
Each of these management theories draws on different aspects of Maslow’s Hierarchy as 
underlying assumptions and motivations. Connect each listed theory with the leadership theory 
or style, and decision-making styles you read about in the previous section.  
Theory X 
McGregor’s Theory X assumes that employees must be controlled, directed, and coerced.
35
 
Other assumptions are that employees have little ambition, they dislike work, are irresponsible, 
and will avoid work, if possible. Managers believing in Theory X think that the primary employee 
motivation is first monetary and then, security. Managers may use hard or soft management 
approaches. Hard management approaches include coercion, implicit threats, 
micromanagement, and tight controls and may result in hostility, employees producing poor 
work on purpose, and extreme demands from labor unions. Soft management approaches are 
permissive in the hopes employees will cooperate but may result in workers wanting greater 
reward if they can do less work.  
Theory Y 
McGregor’s Theory Y assumes that most people not only accept but seek responsibility and 
that exerting physical and mental effort at work is as natural as play or rest.
 35
 Managers 
believing in Theory Y believe that most people will exercise self-control to achieve an 
organizational objective. Theory Y builds on the esteem and self-actualization needs from 
Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs. Note that managing via Theory Y does not mean a manager must 
use a “soft approach”. Key tools for managers using Theory Y include: decentralization and 
delegation, broadening the scope of an employee’s job description, consulting employees in 
decision making (participative management), and having employees set goals and conduct self-
evaluations. 
Theory Z of Ouchi 
Theory Z of Ouchi is a “Japanese Management” style that boomed in the 1980’s. Japanese 
employees were empowered and productive, leading to high quality products at low prices. 
Managerial assumptions about employees were that employees desired affiliation and expected 
support from their company, and that family culture and traditions are important, both at work 
and at home. Extensive training is a tool in Theory Z management to develop a sense of order 
and moral obligation to work hard and produce quality work. Managers believing in Theory Z 
focus on improving employee loyalty to the organization by improving their well-being, both in 
and outside of work. Benefits of this managerial style include stable employment (low turnover), 
high productivity, high morale, and high employee satisfaction.
36
 Companies using this form of 
management typically have slow promotions, group decision-making, and life-time employment 
opportunities.

Organizational Structures 
Unity of Command 
Unity of Command: each employee is accountable to only one superior. Considered to be a 
traditional or classical management approach.
35
  
Scalar Principle 
Scalar Principle: a management rule where each employee reports directly to their higher up 
and no one else. Authority and responsibility flow via delegation in a direct line, vertically from 
highest to lowest levels. This principle is also known as the chain of command.
 35
 
Line Authority 
Line Authority: when multiple employees report to a single administrator, such as 20 clinical 
RDs reporting to one nutrition manager. There is still a chain of command but it is via line 
authority (multiple employees to one supervisor) versus the one-to-one in Unity of Command.
 35
 
Synergy 
Synergy: when the outcome of multiple employees working together is greater than the sum of 
them working alone; you can replace the word “employees” with functional units; it’s a practical 
approach for getting results.
1
  
Managers can foster synergy by creating teams of people who complement each other, facilitate 
collaboration through open work spaces, and set common objectives that let team members 
overcome individual differences. 
Synergy can also be used in marketing and is particularly effective for small businesses or 
departments that lack a large budget for an aggressive marketing campaign. Synergy in 
marketing is when multiple low-cost initiatives are launched, rather than a single large initiative. 
For example, let’s say the bakery department at your hospital wants to increase sales. The 
manager creates a brochure with photos of their pastry trays, fruit trays, and muffin options. She 
sends the brochure to all hospital departments with a special offer to bundle the breakfast 
catering service with discounted coffee service.

In a centralized organization, decision making authority is centralized in upper management.

or business owners and polices are established to ensure consistency throughout the 
organization.  
In a decentralized organization, decision making authority is delegated down to the lower levels

How is Theory Y related to de/centralization? 
Centralization and decentralization are opposites of one another and exist on a spectrum. A 
company or department does not have to be fully centralized or decentralized. A start-up that 
needs adaptability and teamwork would do better with a decentralized structure. On the other 
hand, a large organization, like a pharmaceutical company with headquarters on multiple 
continents, would use a combination of centralized organization and decentralized organization.  
Neither centralization or decentralization is considered effective or ineffective on its own – the 
effectiveness of the organizational structure depends on the attributes of the organization.

Systems Theory 
A system is a set of interdependent parts that work together to achieve a common goal.
1
 
Management is the integration and coordination of resources to achieve the desired objectives 
of the organization. 
Organizations are open systems, influenced by and interacting with external forces in its 
surrounding environment. They are comprised of interdependent parts, or subsystems, that 
interact via feedback, operations / transformations, and in the setting of controls, management, 
and memory. 
Don’t worry about identifying how “systems thinking”, “systems theory”, and “systems analysis” 
are different. Instead, recognize that all 3 terms are key to thinking about organizations as 
systems. They all relate to systems thinking and theory, and you should be aware that different 
terms exist but refer to this overarching concept. 
 
Six Foundational Concepts 
Read about the 6 foundational concepts to systems theory on the next page to help you better 
understand what systems theory is. Think about how a food service manager might implement a 
systems approach in their management style. Don’t worry about memorizing these foundational 
concepts. Use them to provide context.

Six Foundational Concepts of Systems Theory 
1) Organizations are a collection of parts, united by interactions and designed for 
accomplishing specified objectives and goals. 
2) Organizations are interdependent and not self-contained, as they operate within a society. 
3) Organizations are highly complex and attention should be given to the various inputs, 
processes, outputs, feedback loops, and general environment under which and within the 
organization functions. 
4) Organizations constantly interact with the environment that changes them and in return, 
change the environment. 
5) The organization cannot be understood as a function of the isolated parts, because the 
behavior of the system depends on how each part interacts with the others, not what each 
part is doing. 
6) Organizations exist in dynamic equilibrium, that is, they react to internal and external 
changes and forces in ways that create a new state of equilibrium and balance. 
According to systems theory, organizations:  
• are a collection of parts united by 
interactions 
• are designed to accomplish specific 
objectives 
• are interdependent and not self-
contained 
• are highly complex 
• constantly interact with the environment – 
changing both the organization and the 
environment, and existing in dynamic 
equilibrium 
• must be understood by considering all parts  
Takeaways 
• Concepts 1 and 2: Organizations are systems, made up of subsystems, and need to be 
managed. Write the definition of management from Part I. 
• Concepts 3 through 6: The interactions of subsystems, operations, and transformations, 
include feedback with internal and external environments.

1
 
1. DESIRED OUTPUTS: Identify an organization’s fundamental values and the goals and 
objectives that arise from those values  
• Outputs include finished products and services  
2. ENVIRONMENT: Assessment of the organization’s environment as opportunities or threats  
3. INPUTS: Assessment of the organization’s resources and capabilities  
• Inputs include resources like money, materials, time, personnel, facilities, and 
information 
4. OPERATIONS: Identification of an organization’s structure 
• Operations refer to the work done to transform inputs into outputs, also known as 
transformation 
5. MANAGEMENT: Development of the organization’s management structure  
• Functions of management (list them from Part I): 
• Linking processes: communication, decision making

Transformation: the processes required to change inputs into outputs

Systems thinking is a management approach that thinks about the organizational unit as a 
whole when making decisions or allocating resources. 
Subsystems are complete systems unto themselves but are also part of a larger system. 
Departments are examples of subsystems. There can be tension and conflict when the 
activities of two subsystems overlap.
1
 This can occur when there are no boundaries or there is a 
permeability of boundaries. 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
52 
 
Example: Patient Tray Service 
 
Food and nutrition is responsible for delivering patient 
trays and nursing is responsible for patient care. 
However, the interface between nursing and food and 
nutrition service, regarding all the details of patient tray 
service, isn’t very clear. There are no official boundaries 
communicated by either department in terms of activity 
limitations. This situation leads to clashes between staff 
in the 2 subsystems. 
 
Example: Cold Food and Feedback -The dietetic technicians and dietitians hear from the 
patients and cafeteria customers that the food is cold. The DTRs and RDNs relay this 
information to the food service manager, who discusses recipe changes with the chef, trickling 
down to the line cooks. Using a systems approach, evaluating the effectiveness of a new policy 
should consider both the internal and the external environments via feedback (See next page 
for a diagram).  
In this example, internal feedback comes from the patients and customers about food 
temperature to the diet tech or the dietitian. Internal feedback occurs within a department, so the 
communication between the DTR/RDNs, foodservice manager, chef, and line cooks is also 
internal feedback. 
External feedback, such as that from The Joint Commission or the health inspector, is external 
feedback. In this example, there is no external feedback. Don’t be fooled by thinking the 
feedback from the patients and customers is external feedback.

Marketing 
Marketing is concerned with:
 1

The 4 P’s of Marketing 
Marketing mix is an effective way to understand what products or services can offer and how to 
present the product successfully. There are 4 P’s of Marketing (mix):
40
 
1. Price: pricing affects how a product is sold; linked to perceived value of product; must 
understand how a consumer sees the product because a price too high OR too low will 
hinder sales. 
2. Promotion: marketing communication strategies and techniques, including advertising, 
sales promotions, special offers, and public relations. Must be suitable for product, price, 
and end user. Promotion is the communication part of the entire marketing function. 
3. Place: how is the product provided to the customer? Placement strategy assesses the 
most suitable channel and must complement the rest of the product strategy. 
4. Product: a tangible good or intangible service; developed to meet a specific consumer 
need and demand. Need to understand the problem the product is trying to solve and 
then convey the benefits to the consumer.

Marketing Lifestyle Groups 
There are 8 VALS (Values and Lifestyles) Segments of American Adult consumers (Innovators, 
Survivors, Makers, Experiencers, Strivers, Achievers, Believers, and Thinkers). They describe 
mindsets by psychographic traits and demographics in how they drive consumer behavior.
41
 
The 2 important aspects of each term are primary motivation and resources. Their combination 
determines how each person expresses themselves in the marketplace as a consumer. Be able 
to match each term with their descriptors. 
We describe VALS groups in terms of primary motivation and resources. 
Primary motivations include ideals, achievement, and self-expression. Innovators are 
motivated by all 3 to different degrees, while survivors have no primary motivations. Thinkers 
and Believers are motivated by ideals. Achievers and Strivers are motivated by achievement. 
Experiencers and Makers are motivated by self-expression. 
Resources go beyond income and include energy, self-confidence, intellectualism, novelty 
seeking, innovativeness, impulsiveness, leadership, and vanity. Different levels of resources 
improve or constrain how a person expresses their primary motivation.

Think about how Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs fits in with these marketing lifestyle groups. 
• Innovators: have high income, high self-esteem. Successful and sophisticated, take-
charge. Abundant resources and exhibit all 3 primary motivations: ideals, achievements, 
self-expression 
• Survivors: live narrowly-focused lives; believe the world is changing too quickly as they 
have few resources with which to cope; comfortable with the familiar and concerned with 
safety and security; do not have strong primary motivation because they are focused on 
meeting needs, not fulfilling desires 
• Makers: have lower income; focus on self-sufficiency and family; express themselves 
and experience the world by working on it – building a house, raising children, fixing a 
car, home-canning vegetables; have the skill and energy to carry out their projects 
• Experiencers: are young, impulsive; seek variety, excitement, and risky activities; have 
self-expression, enthusiasm– quickly become enthusiastic about new possibilities but 
equally quickly move on  
• Strivers: trendy, fun loving, motivated by achievement and concerned about the 
opinions and approval of others; they define their success by money 
• Achievers: successful individuals, typically high income, motivated by a desire for 
achievement, have goal-oriented lifestyles, and a deep commitment to career and family 
• Believers: motivated by ideals, conservative and conventional, concrete beliefs based 
on traditional codes – family, religion, community, the nation 
• Thinkers: motivated by ideals, mature, satisfied, comfortable, and reflective. Value 
order, knowledge, and responsibility.

Cultural competence: a set of congruent attitudes, behaviors, and policies that come together;

Shortcomings of cultural competence include the tendency to center “whiteness” as

Cultural humility: the ability to maintain an interpersonal stance that is “other-oriented” (open

The ETHNIC Model of culturally competent care 
How does a patient’s understanding of their illness and treatment fit with what is expected and 
accepted in their culture? How can we determine treatment options in a collaborative (not 
imposed) process? This model focuses on questions and conversations providers should have 
with patients to achieve their goals. The model stands for: Explanation, Treatment, Healers, 
Negotiation, Intervention, Collaboration. (Steven J Levin, MD; Robert C Like, MD; Jan E 
Gottleib, MPH 1997)

The LEARN model was designed to overcome communication barriers in health care 
encounters. The model stands for: Listen, Explain, Acknowledge, Recommend treatment, 
Negotiate agreement. (Elois Ann Berlin, William C. Fowlkes, Jr in 1983)

This model helps create a culturally competent environment, focusing on the psychosocial 
context for the patient’s visit rather than medical details. The model stands for: Background, 
Affect, Trouble, Handling, Empathy. (Marian Stuart, PhD and Joseph A Lieberman III, MD MPH

Acculturation is the assimilation to a different culture.  
 
Acclimation is the process or result of becoming accustomed to a new climate or conditions. 
 
 
Campinha-Bacote Model 
Starting in the individual, this model states that cultural competence expands to the family and 
community level. The 5-step process focuses on 1) awareness, 2) knowledge, 3) skill, 4) 
encounter, and 5) desire. 
Awareness involves challenging assumptions and asking client questions about their values, 
beliefs, and practices, resulting in acquisition of cultural knowledge. With cultural knowledge, 
the provider familiarizes themselves with variations in cultural impact and expands their cultural 
skills to collect key information in order to conduct effective assessments and decide on an 
intervention that takes into account preferred language, foods consumed or avoided (especially 
when feeling ill), and foods consumed or avoided for religious reasons.  
This cultural encounter is the best time to discuss nutrition. The final step is cultural desire, 
where the practitioners want to engage in cross-cultural care. (Joseph Campinha-Bacote, PhD 
1998) 
Know the definitions of the 5 steps. 
The Purnell Model 
This model is derived from multiple theories and includes 16 assumptions about culture. “A 
culturally competent health care provider develops an awareness of his or her existence, 
sensations, thoughts, and environment without letting these factors have an undue effect on 
those for whom care is provided.” – Larry Purnell, PhD RN FAAN. 
Explanatory Models Approach 
This model “eschews the trait list approach that understands culture as a set of already known 
factors and instead, emphasizes engagement with others and with the practices that people 
undertake in their local worlds and the ambivalence that many people feel as a result of being 
between worlds, including those who self-identify with multiple cultural groups.” It is a 6-step 
“mini-ethnography” approach that has health care providers use their expert knowledge next to 
the patient’s own explanation and viewpoint.  
(Arthur Kleinman, MD and Peter Benson, MD)

Continuous quality improvement programs:

CQI is an ongoing cycle of collecting data and using 
the data to make decisions for gradual and 
continuous improvement to programs and

49
 
CQI takes a broad look at the overall system within 
which different programs and processes operate, 
and keeps that system in mind when identifying problems, implementing corrective action, and 
studying the effectiveness of the solution. Does that sound like systems theory? 
 
The key tenants of CQI are: 
• Customer satisfaction 
• Data collection via the scientific method

Customer satisfaction directs the CQI process towards what should be examined. Data

that the improved program or process might touch.  
A non-nutrition example of CQI is how the motor vehicle industry develops new versions of cars 
each year, incorporating improvements based on user experience studies (such as adding 
technology and comfort), environmental concerns (hybrid and electric cars), and changing

Mustang!

manufacturing or packaging facility. How can managers make effective changes 
if they never observe and participate in how things are actually done?  
4. Empower people – set the same goals for teams and provide a system and tools 
so they can reach their goals.  
5. Be transparent – use real data to show tangible and visible performance 
improvement results

FADE Quality Improvement Model 
The FADE Quality Improvement Model, sometimes called the FADE Performance Model, was 
established to improve healthcare quality. 
The FADE model incorporates 4 key aspects of effective quality improvement initiatives:
52
 
1) Considers both systems and processes 
2) Focuses on patients 
3) Focuses on being part of the team 
4) Focuses on the use of data 
You can see that with aspects 1, 3, and 4, the FADE model is similar to the CQI model, which 
makes sense because they are both focused on quality improvement.  
FADE stands for Focus, Analyze, Develop, and Execute. 
 
We focus by generating a list of problems, selecting 1 problem, and then verifying and defining 
the problem. We analyze by deciding what we need to know, collecting data baselines and 
patterns, and determining influential factors. We develop by generating promising solutions, 
selecting solutions, and developing implementation plans. Finally, we execute by gaining 
commitment from the team, executing the plan, and monitoring impact.

Project Management Tools 
Pareto Chart 
In the FOCUS model section, the diagram mentions Pareto Charts and cause and effect charts 
in “F” for “Find the problem”. Pareto charts have 2 components: a bar graph that represents the 
descending frequency of occurrence (or cost), and a line graph representing the cumulative 
percentage of total occurrences (or cost). 
Why do we use Pareto charts? By ordering the occurrence of a complaint in descending order, 
we can identify the complaint that has the largest effect on satisfaction.
35
 We can also determine 
the number of factors we need to address in order to reduce complaint frequency by a certain 
percent. If the chart represents cost rather than occurrence, then you can imagine that 
addressing the most expensive problem, or knowing how many problems you need to address 
to cut costs to meet your budget, would be helpful.  
Example: Diner Complaints - The chart tabulates the number of diner complaints in 
categories: impolite server, difficulty getting server’s attention, automatic gratuity of 18%, 
inaccurate check calculations, incorrect meals, dirty utensils or tables, slow service, serving cold 
meals (incorrect temperature), and loud or distracting diners at other tables. On the secondary 
axis, we have the cumulative percent of each complaint. We use the cumulative percent to 
figure out that if we want to reduce complaints by 75% (horizontal line), we should address the 
top 3 factors – impolite servicers, difficulty getting servers attention, and automatic 18% gratuity, 
because if we follow the thick line corresponding to the secondary access, we reach about 70% 
after the first 3 categories. If we didn’t construct this Pareto chart, we might instead focus on 
other factors on the list based on our subjective improvement, and then not see the 
improvement in complaints we are looking for. It’s all about collecting data to fix your problem. 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
86 
 
PERT Chart 
PERT chart stands for “Program Evaluation Review Technique” and is a project management 
evaluation tool used for effective planning at the management level. We use it to schedule, 
organize, and coordinate tasks within a project.
 35
 
To construct a PERT chart: 
1) Each task necessary to complete a project is written in nodes (blank boxes in this example). 
2) Directional arrows represent sequential tasks – one must be completed before another. Not 
all tasks are sequential; some can be performed at the same time. 
3) The amount of time needed to complete each activity is written along each line – it could be 
days, weeks, hours, or months. 
4) Critical path analysis is a bolded line that shows the longest path by duration to complete 
the project as described in the chart. 
Nodes are key tasks or milestones, and might be a rectangle or a circle, with the task written 
inside. Or, the node might have a number and the tasks listed in a separate table. Parallel tasks 
without direct arrows going from one task to another can occur at the same time. Breaking a 
project down into necessary tasks is a key step in effective project management in general. 
Creating sequences between the tasks is helpful, especially if different people are working on 
those tasks. Critical path analysis provides you, as the manager, and your boss, with an idea of 
the longest time the process might take.  
PERT charts may also be used to work backwards from a hard deadline. We would subtract the 
amount of time needed for each activity to get our start date. 
Finally, PERT charts are helpful for project managers to evaluate and analyze which resources 
(labor, time, materials) are needed for a project, as well as track the progress of the project. If a 
project includes multiple departments, having a master PERT chart can help facilitate 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
87 
 
communication and make sure everyone is on the same page, especially if a task in your 
department is dependent on a task in another department.  
The time component of the PERT chart is invaluable. As a manager, you can estimate the 
shortest time the project might take, the longest, and “what-if” analyses, like “what if step 2 
takes longer than we expect?” Any step or task that has a large impact on the final deadline is a 
“risk” and should be carefully monitored. 
Note that the time estimates for PERT charts are just that – estimates. It’s a planning procedure 
and is better than not considering time at all, but the time requirement isn’t guaranteed. PERT 
charts work best if you have detailed knowledge of the tasks and processes that will be involved 
and can easily fill out the components. If a large amount of research is required to determine 
duration and cost, and the time spent on that research isn’t offset by the benefit of the PERT 
chart, then another project planning tool may be a better choice. 
Cause and Effect Diagram 
Cause and effect diagrams are also called “Ishikawa” diagrams, named after their developer, 
Kaoru Ishikawa, or “Fishbone Diagrams’ because of the format.  
These diagrams can be used to identify problems for continuous quality improvement programs, 
like the F step of the FOCUS model.
54
 It can also be used in less formal processes to discover 
bottlenecks and root causes of problems.  
To construct a cause and effect diagram: 
1) Write down the problem to one side as the fish head 
2) Brainstorm major factors that might affect the situation by drawing lines off the center 
spine 
3) Write down causes (horizontal lines) that contribute to each major factor 
4) Add further sub-causes with more branches off of the cause lines (vertical) and their 
factors (horizontal) 
5) Analyze the diagram 

 
© 2022 Veritense™

An advantage of cause-and-effect diagrams is that they often reveal causes that are not obvious 
at first, and if addressed, lead to effective solutions. 
What would the diagram look like if you only had major factors and causes (no sub causes or 
sub-factors)? What if you had sub-causes and factors for each node? 
Planning Processes 
There are several planning processes that may be used in keyword questions on the exam. 
These include: 
• Delphi method 
• Delbecq’s nominal group technique 
• Charettes 
Delphi method: a group of experts are contacted individually and anonymously with 
questionnaires.
55
 Their responses are statistically tallied and summarized in a report which is 
sent back to the experts with another questionnaire. The goal of the Delphi method is to reduce 
the range of responses from experts and arrive at what might be called a consensus. 
 
Delbecq’s nominal group technique: using 
structured, small-group discussion to reach a 
consensus.
56
 The moderator presents the 
question of interest. Each group member writes 
down their ideas separately. Then each person 
shares their idea and the moderator writes them 
on a group board. The group then discusses 
each idea, revising them, and then votes on the 
best one. 
Charrette: collaborative planning process harnessing the talents of all interested parties.
57
 
Results in a master plan that represents transformative community change.

Lean Six Sigma 
As you read through this section, jot down your own study guide questions. What 
information are main takeaways? Turn that answer around into a question.  
Lean 
“Lean” is a “systematic approach to eliminate activities that don’t add value to a 
process”
58
 with the goal of high quality and customer satisfaction. Waste removal is a 
continuous process. To use Lean, you have to define value – or what the customer’s 
perception of the product or service is and what they are willing to pay for. You can then 
label work activities as value-added or non-value-added.  
Lean evolved from the Toyota Production System and uses the 3M model of Muda, 
Mura, and Muri to expose inefficient processes.
58
  
Muda is any activity that does not add value to a product or service, and is the 
Japanese word for waste. There are 8 types of Muda: defects, overproduction, 
waiting, non-utilized talents, transportation, inventory, motion, extra processing 
→ it spells DOWNTIME. 
Mura is the concept of inconsistencies in business operations that results in 
production fluctuations, and thus suboptimal resource utilization. Uneven 
workloads, inventory accumulation, and waiting are examples of Muda that are 
Mura. These are particularly applicable to a foodservice setting. What 
foodservice system type has an uneven distribution of food preparation work? 
What measures do we use to quantify the value of products in inventory?  
Muri is the occurrence of dissatisfaction amongst people or unused resources, 
such as machinery. Customers left waiting, ovens not being utilized, and staff 
being over stressed are examples of Muri. Muri leads to Muda. 
The 5 Principles of Lean are: 1) Identify your customers and what they value; 2) map 
the value stream; 3) create flow to the customer; 4) establish pull based on customer 
demand, and 5) seek continuous improvement.
59
 
Lean approaches use tools like the PDSA model, 5S (Sort, Set in Order, Shine, 
Standardize, Sustain), and 8 wastes. 
Six Sigma 
The goal of Six Sigma is to create effective processes. Six Sigma itself is a problem-
solving methodology that uses data and the DMAIC framework – define, measure, 
analyze, improve, and control.
 58
 If you’ve read through the previous sections in this 
study guide then this framework should look familiar. 

 
© 2022 Veritense™ 
90 
 
• Define – Define the problem and objectives. This may be done with a problem 
statement or goal statement, as well as listing out the scope of the project, 
necessary resources, anticipated benefits, and the project timeline. 
• Measure – What do we need to improve, and can we measure it? Identify and 
measure baseline metrics to compare to final performance later. 
• Analyze – Analyze the process and define the factors of influence. Conduct “root 
cause analysis” with Pareto charts and fishbone diagrams. Verify and validate 
root causes with statistical analyses. 
• Improve – Identify and implement improvements. Form solutions and test them. 
Do they eliminate the root causes? In this stage, you may run simulation studies 
or pilot interventions.  
• Control – Ensure that improvements will be sustained. Record the performance 
of the solutions implemented in the Improve step, and put a control system in 
place to monitor performance. Standardized processes using instructions and 
verify estimated project benefits against anticipated benefits.  
Lean Six Sigma 
Lean Six Sigma combines the waste-reduction goals of Lean with the problem-solving 
methodology of Six Sigma. First steps may use Lean to make a workplace efficient and 
effective, using less technical tools like KAIZEN and workplace organization, followed 
by Six Sigma statistical analyses to identify root causes for any problems that remain.
60
 
You can think of Lean Six Sigma in terms of 3 elements: customers, processes, and 
employees.
58
 Customers are anyone who is consuming a business’ products or 
services. Processes are core to Lean, with inefficient processes leading to poor quality 
and profit losses. Finally, similar to how KAIZEN™ emphasizes involving managers and 
workers in changes, successful Lean Six Sigma initiatives or infrastructure changes 
require everyone in the organization to be involved and invested. 
Now that you’ve read through this material, go online and search for videos on Lean Six 
Sigma to help learn the information in a different way.

Answer: B. quality assurance (QA) programs are typically individual-focused, rather than systems- or 
process-focused, and involves measuring compliance against standards. You can think about QA in 
contrast to quality improvement (QI). QA is often defensive or reactive, while QI is proactive and 
continuous, focused on exploring the problem and potential solutions. QA is focused on determining if 
employees or teams are adhering to standardized procedures and meeting benchmarks.

model incorporates 4 key aspects of effective quality improvement initiatives: Considers both systems and 
processes, focuses on patients, focuses on being part of the team, and Focuses on the use of data.
